sourceQuestion:
Let R be a non-empty relation on a collection of sets defined by A R B if and only if A ∩ B = ∅. Then, (pick the true statement)
A. R is reflexive and transitive
B. R is symmetric and not transitive
C. R is an equivalence relation
D. R is not reflexive and not symmetric
sourceAnswer: B. R is symmetric and not transitive

parsed.stem: Let R be a non-empty relation on a collection of sets defined by A R B if and only if A ∩ B = ∅. Then, (pick the true statement)

options.text: R is symmetric and not transitive
R is not reflexive and not symmetric

D. Equal to n!

sourceQuestion:
Match the following pairs:
A. Number of distinct binary trees with n nodes
B. Number of binary strings of length 2n with an equal number of 0s and 1s
C. Number of even permutations of n objects
D. Number of binary strings of length 2n which are palindromes

P. C(2n, n)
Q. C(2n, n) / (n+1)
R. n! / 2
S. (1/(n+1)) * C(2n, n)

Which of the following is the correct matching?
A. A-Q, B-P, C-R, D-S
B. A-S, B-P, C-R, D-Q
C. A-Q, B-S, C-R, D-P
D. A-P, B-Q, C-S, D-R

sourceQuestion:
Let R_1 and R_2 be two equivalence relations on a set. Consider the following assertions:
i. R_1 ∩ R_2 is an equivalence relation
ii. R_1 ∪ R_2 is an equivalence relation

Which of the following is correct?
A. Both assertions are true
B. Assertions (i) is true but assertions (ii) is not true
C. Assertions (ii) is true but assertions (i) is not true
D. Neither (i) nor (ii) is true
sourceAnswer: C. Assertions (ii) is true but assertions (i) is not true

parsed.stem:
Let R_1 and R_2 be two equivalence relations on a set. Consider the following assertions:
i. R_1 ∩ R_2 is an equivalence relation
ii. R_1 ∪ R_2 is an equivalence relation

Which of the following is correct?

options.text: Assertions (i) is true but assertions (ii) is not true
Assertions (ii) is true but assertions (i) is not true

sourceQuestion:
The binary relation R = {(1,1),(2,1),(2,2),(2,3),(2,4),(3,1),(3,2),(4,1),(4,2)} on the set A = {1,2,3,4} is:
A. reflexive, symmetric and transitive
B. neither reflexive, nor irreflexive but transitive
C. irreflexive, symmetric and transitive
D. irreflexive and antisymmetric
sourceAnswer: B. neither reflexive, nor irreflexive but transitive

options.text: neither reflexive, nor irreflexive but transitive

sourceQuestion:
A relation R is defined on the set of integers as xRy iff (x + y) is even. Which of the following statements is true?

A. R is not an equivalence relation
B. R is an equivalence relation having 1 equivalence class
C. R is an equivalence relation having 2 equivalence classes
D. R is an equivalence relation having 3 equivalence classes
sourceAnswer: C. R is an equivalence relation having 2 equivalence classes

parsed.stem: A relation R is defined on the set of integers as xRy iff (x + y) is even. Which of the following statements is true?

options.text: R is not an equivalence relation
R is an equivalence relation having 1 equivalence class
R is an equivalence relation having 2 equivalence classes
R is an equivalence relation having 3 equivalence classes

sourceQuestion:
Consider the following relations:

R1: (a,b)R1(c,d) iff (a-c) is even, over the set of integers
R2: (a,b)R2(c,d) iff (a-c) is odd, over the set of integers
R3: (a,b)R3(c,d) iff a/c is rational, over the set of non-zero rational numbers
R4: (a,b)R4(c,d) iff a - b <= 2, over the set of natural numbers

Which of the following statements is correct?

A. R1 and R3 are equivalence relations, R2 and R4 are not
B. R1 and R2 are equivalence relations, R3 and R4 are not
C. R1, R2 and R3 are equivalence relations, R4 is not
D. R1, R2, R3 and R4 are all equivalence relations
sourceAnswer: B. R1 and R2 are equivalence relations, R3 and R4 are not

parsed.stem:
Consider the following relations:

R1: (a,b)R1(c,d) iff (a-c) is even, over the set of integers
R2: (a,b)R2(c,d) iff (a-c) is odd, over the set of integers
R3: (a,b)R3(c,d) iff a/c is rational, over the set of non-zero rational numbers
R4: (a,b)R4(c,d) iff a - b <= 2, over the set of natural numbers

Which of the following statements is correct?

options.text: R1 and R3 are equivalence relations, R2 and R4 are not
R1 and R2 are equivalence relations, R3 and R4 are not
R1, R2 and R3 are equivalence relations, R4 is not
R1, R2, R3 and R4 are all equivalence relations

A. Neither reflexive nor symmetric
B. Symmetric and reflexive
C. Transitive and reflexive
D. Transitive and symmetric

sourceQuestion:
Let R and S be any two equivalence relations on a non-empty set A. Which one of the following statements is TRUE?

A. R union S and R intersect S are both equivalence relations
B. R union S is an equivalence relation
C. R intersect S is an equivalence relation
D. Neither R union S nor R intersect S are equivalence relations
sourceAnswer: C. R intersect S is an equivalence relation

R union S is an equivalence relation
R intersect S is an equivalence relation
Neither R union S nor R intersect S are equivalence relations

C. O(n^(3/2))
D. O(n^3)

sourceQuestion:
A relation R is defined on ordered pairs of integers as follows: (x,y)R(u,v) if x < u and y > v. Then R is:

A. Neither a Partial Order nor an Equivalence Relation
B. A Partial Order but not a Total Order
C. A total Order
D. An Equivalence Relation
sourceAnswer: A. Neither a Partial Order nor an Equivalence Relation

D. n and 1

sourceQuestion:
Consider the binary relation R = {(x,y),(y,x),(z,z)} on the set {x,y,z}. Which one of the following is TRUE?
A. R is symmetric but NOT antisymmetric
B. R is NOT symmetric but antisymmetric
C. R is both symmetric and antisymmetric
D. R is neither symmetric nor antisymmetric
sourceAnswer: A. R is symmetric but NOT antisymmetric

B. 2^15
C. 2^20
D. 2^25

sourceQuestion:
Let R be the relation on the set of positive integers such that aRb if and only if a and b are distinct and have a common divisor other than 1. Which one of the following statements about R is true?
A. R is symmetric and reflexive but not transitive
B. R is reflexive but not symmetric and not transitive
C. R is transitive but not symmetric
D. R is symmetric but neither reflexive nor transitive
sourceAnswer: D. R is symmetric but neither reflexive nor transitive

parsed.stem: Let R be the relation on the set of positive integers such that aRb if and only if a and b are distinct and have a common divisor other than 1. Which one of the following statements about R is true?

options.text: R is symmetric and reflexive but not transitive
R is reflexive but not symmetric and not transitive
R is transitive but not symmetric
R is symmetric but neither reflexive nor transitive

sourceQuestion:
Let R be a relation on the set of ordered pairs of positive integers such that ((p,q),(r,s)) is in R if and only if p - q = s - r. Which one of the following is true about R?
A. Both reflexive and symmetric
B. Reflexive but not symmetric
C. Not reflexive but symmetric
D. Neither reflexive nor symmetric

sourceQuestion:
A binary relation R on N x N is defined as follows: (a,b) R (c,d) if a <= c or b <= d. Consider the following propositions:
P: R is reflexive.
Q: R is transitive.
Which one of the following statements is TRUE?
A. Both P and Q are true.
B. P is true and Q is false.
C. P is false and Q is true.
D. Both P and Q are false.
sourceAnswer: B. P is true and Q is false.

parsed.stem:
A binary relation R on N x N is defined as follows: (a,b) R (c,d) if a <= c or b <= d. Consider the following propositions:
P: R is reflexive.
Q: R is transitive.
Which one of the following statements is TRUE?

options.text: Both P and Q are true.
P is true and Q is false.
P is false and Q is true.
Both P and Q are false.

sourceQuestion:
A relation R on a set S is said to be circular if aRb and bRc together imply cRa. Which of the following options is/are correct?

A. If a relation R is reflexive and symmetric, then R is an equivalence relation.
B. If a relation R is circular and symmetric, then R is an equivalence relation.
C. If a relation R is reflexive and circular, then R is an equivalence relation.
D. If a relation R is transitive and circular, then R is an equivalence relation.
sourceAnswer: C. If a relation R is reflexive and circular, then R is an equivalence relation.

parsed.stem: A relation R on a set S is said to be circular if aRb and bRc together imply cRa. Which of the following options is/are correct?

options.text: If a relation R is reflexive and symmetric, then R is an equivalence relation.
If a relation R is circular and symmetric, then R is an equivalence relation.
If a relation R is reflexive and circular, then R is an equivalence relation.
If a relation R is transitive and circular, then R is an equivalence relation.

sourceQuestion:
Let F be the set of all functions from {1, 2, ..., n} to {0, 1}. Define the binary relation ~ on F as follows: f ~ g if and only if f(x) <= g(x), where 0 <= 1. Which of the following statement(s) is/are TRUE?

A. ~ is a symmetric relation
B. ~ is a partial order
C. ~ is a lattice
D. ~ is an equivalence relation

Let R_1 be a relation from A = {2, 3, 4} to B = {2, 4, 6, 8} and R_2 be another relation from B = {2, 4, 6, 8} to C = {1, 2, 3, 4} as defined below:

i. An element x in A is related to an element y in B (under R_1) if y is divisible by x.
ii. An element y in B is related to an element z in C (under R_2) if z is even but not divisible by y.

Which is the composite relation R_1 R_2 from A to C?

sourceQuestion:
Let S be an infinite set and S_1, S_2, ..., S_n be sets such that S_1 ∪ S_2 ∪ ... ∪ S_n = S. Then
A. at least one of the sets S_i is a finite set
B. not more than one of the sets S_i can be finite
C. at least one of the sets S_i is an infinite set
D. not more than one of the sets S_i can be infinite
E. None of the above
sourceAnswer: C. at least one of the sets S_i is an infinite set

parsed.stem: Let S be an infinite set and S_1, S_2, ..., S_n be sets such that S_1 ∪ S_2 ∪ ... ∪ S_n = S. Then

options.text: at least one of the sets S_i is a finite set
not more than one of the sets S_i can be finite
at least one of the sets S_i is an infinite set
not more than one of the sets S_i can be infinite

D. (2^2)^n
E. None of the above
